text: The surgeon attempts to unfold the transverse colon mesentery, suspecting a paraduodenal hernia beneath it. The transverse colon appears adhered to the descending colon, complicating identification of the anatomical structures. The surgeon proceeds with lateral dissection around the splenic flexure to clarify the anatomy.

The surgeon identifies the transverse colon and the omentum on the distal transverse colon, proceeding to dissect and take down the splenic flexure. The anatomy in the left upper quadrant appears complex, with visible bowel loops complicating the dissection process.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection to medialize the bowel, carefully navigating the complex anatomy to avoid misidentifying structures and inadvertently removing mesentery that should remain intact.

The surgeon unfolds the mesentery to better identify anatomical structures. The tattoo is visible on the proximal descending colon, and the splenic flexure is being lowered. However, there is an unclear arrangement of bowel in the left upper quadrant.

The surgeon examines the attachment between the transverse colon and descending colon, marked by a tattoo, attempting to separate and unfold these structures to better define the anatomy. The mesentery is visible beneath, and a hernia defect is observed underneath the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), with small bowel positioned above the splenic flexure in the left upper quadrant.
The surgeon observes a large defect in the abdominal cavity, attempting to reduce the bowel and locate the ligament of Treitz.

The surgeon observes that the splenic flexure is positioned downward and proceeds to separate the transverse colon from the descending colon, addressing unusual congenital attachments to preserve as much bowel as possible.

The surgeon identifies a defect beneath the mesentery and proceeds to take down lateral attachments to straighten the bowel. The colon mesentery is placed under medial tension while dissection continues over the kidney, with the left ureter visible in the distance.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the last of the lateral attachments of the descending colon leading up to the flexure. The ligament of Treitz is visible laterally, and the transverse colon omentum is observed at the distal transverse colon, with some of it being dissected down.

The surgeon performs further lateral mobilization of the colon to remove tension from the lateral attachments, revealing the proper orientation of the bowel. The redundancy of the bowel is noted, and the surgeon identifies the distal margin for resection, focusing on the proximal descending colon where the tumor is located.

The surgeon observes the sigmoid colon with robust lateral adhesions to the pelvic sidewall, indicating a lateral to medial approach for mobilization. The colon is now fully mobilized, preparing for mesenteric transaction and planning proximal and distal margins.

The surgeon holds the proximal colon with the left hand while dissecting the mesentery along the sub-IMV region. The thick tissues are manipulated, likely due to chronic friction from bowel herniation, without extensive dissection of the blood vessels.

The surgeon dissects the left colic artery to preserve the superior hemorrhoidal artery, as the resection is focused on the splenic flexure. The dissection occurs beneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery, with the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) visible along the aorta. The left colic artery is traced and dissected off the IMA.

The surgeon dissects the superior hemorrhoidal artery and the left colic artery, positioned superiorly. After freeing these structures, the surgeon prepares to use a vessel sealer. Sigmoidal branches are visible extending from the dissected area.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to take the left colic branch and traces it up to the chosen distal margin in the mid sigmoid colon. The camera is repositioned to the second port for better visualization, while a twelve millimeter trocar is placed as the third arm through the rectus muscle to minimize hernia risk and pain at the port site.

The surgeon prepares to perform a sewn anastomosis after completing the bowel division. Two OPDS are used to join the two staple lines, which are aligned neatly. The camera is positioned at port number three for optimal visualization.

The surgeon uses two left hands and one right hand to align two staple lines for the anastomosis. The colotomies are made, and the suture is tied to itself. The ICG fluorescence is checked, confirming good perfusion.

The surgeon performs the inner layer of the anastomosis using three OV lock sutures, starting in the middle and running down towards the camera. The two sutures are brought together in a canal fashion and tied. The outer anterior row is completed with two OPDS sutures, finalizing a two-layer anastomosis.

The surgeon prepares for a natural orifice extraction of the bowel specimen by placing an EEA sizer into the vagina, held in place by a nurse. A vaginotomy is made, followed by the introduction of a fifteen millimeter endocatch bag to contain the cancerous specimen. The bag is closed and removed, allowing the bowel to unfold and be extracted through the small vaginotomy without significant tearing or stretching.

The surgeon closes the surgical site in two layers, starting with the mucosa and muscular layer, followed by the peritoneum. The closure is performed through the open peritoneum, ensuring the layers are securely sutured.
The surgeon performs a final inspection of the surgical field, highlighting the mobilized splenic flexure and the ligament of Treitz.
